title: Category Balance and Overlap Auditor
description: Given a sample 12-category taxonomy with one deliberately over-represented category (38%) and two categories with an ambiguous shared boundary, the learner can identify the balance violation from the bar chart alone and, after inspecting the flagged category pair, propose which single criterion would resolve the overlap.
creator: Learning Graphs
author: Dan McCreary
subject: Learning Graphs

educational.topic: Category Balance, Category Overlap, Taxonomy Validation
learningObjectives: Identify a category-balance violation from a distribution chart and name the threshold it crosses
Explain why a category past roughly 30% should be split rather than trimmed
Recognize that two well-balanced categories can still have a defective boundary
Propose a disambiguating criterion and test it against every ambiguous concept

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Chart-based critique paired with a propose-and-compare boundary exercise
keyQuestions: Which category is over the ceiling, and why is splitting it a better fix than moving concepts out?
Metadata & Standards and Taxonomy & Classification are both well under 30%. What tells you they have a problem?
Does your criterion decide all three ambiguous concepts, or only two?
Why does 'is it about classifying things?' fail as a disambiguating criterion?
commonMisconceptions: That a balanced taxonomy is a correct taxonomy — the two overlap categories are balanced and still defective
That an over-represented category should be fixed by moving concepts elsewhere rather than by splitting it
That structural graph validation catches taxonomy problems — it catches neither balance nor overlap
That a criterion which resolves one ambiguous concept has resolved the overlap
assessmentOpportunities: Ask learners to name the balance violation and its threshold from the chart alone
Ask whether the two gold categories have a problem, judging from the chart only, before opening the panel
Have learners test their proposed criterion aloud against all three ambiguous concepts
